questions.prompt: Which body system consists of the skin, hair, and nails?
questions.why: Skin and its accessory structures are part of the integumentary system.
questions.trap: Digestive and urinary systems process nutrients and wastes, not body covering.

questions.prompt: Which body system is responsible for the breakdown and absorption of nutrients?
questions.why: The digestive tract breaks down food and absorbs nutrients.
questions.trap: Endocrine releases hormones; nervous transmits impulses.

questions.prompt: Which body system is primarily involved in hormone production?
questions.why: Endocrine glands secrete hormones into the bloodstream.
questions.trap: Skeletal and muscular systems provide support/movement, not hormones.

questions.prompt: What anatomical term refers to the back of the body?
questions.why: Posterior (dorsal) means toward the back.
questions.trap: Anterior (ventral) means toward the front.

questions.prompt: What do you call the state of dynamic equilibrium of the body’s internal environment?
questions.why: Homeostasis is maintaining stable internal conditions.
questions.trap: Metabolism is all chemical reactions; osmosis is water movement.

questions.prompt: What is the study of the structure of the body called?
questions.why: Anatomy focuses on structure; physiology focuses on function.
questions.trap: Pathology is the study of disease.

questions.prompt: What are the smallest living units that make up the body’s structure?
questions.why: Cells are the basic units of life.
questions.trap: Tissues are groups of cells.

questions.prompt: Which type of tissue covers or lines the body’s surfaces?
questions.why: Epithelium covers surfaces and lines cavities.
questions.trap: Connective supports/binds rather than covers/lines.

questions.prompt: Which type of tissue generates and transmits impulses to regulate body functions?
questions.why: Neurons generate and transmit electrical impulses.
questions.trap: Cartilage is supportive connective tissue, not signaling tissue.

questions.prompt: What major body cavity encompasses the front of the body?
questions.why: The ventral cavity includes thoracic and abdominopelvic cavities.
questions.trap: The dorsal cavity includes cranial and vertebral cavities.

questions.prompt: Which particle in the nucleus of an atom has no electrical charge?
questions.why: Neutrons are neutral; protons are positive; electrons are negative.
questions.trap: Cations are charged atoms/molecules, not subatomic particles.

questions.prompt: What do you call a substance that cannot be broken down into two or more simpler substances?
questions.why: Elements are fundamental substances made of one type of atom.
questions.trap: Compounds are made of multiple elements and can be separated chemically.

questions.prompt: What is a chemical combination of two or more different elements called?
questions.why: Compounds form by chemical bonding between elements.
questions.trap: Mixtures are physical combinations without bonding.

questions.prompt: What is an ion with a negative charge called?
questions.why: Anions carry a negative charge; cations are positive.
questions.trap: Neutrons are neutral and not ions.

questions.prompt: How are ionic bonds formed?

choices.A: By transfer of electrons creating oppositely charged ions that attract
choices.D: By physical mixing without chemical change

questions.answer: By transfer of electrons creating oppositely charged ions that attract
questions.why: Electron transfer produces ions; opposite charges attract.
questions.trap: Sharing electrons describes covalent bonds.

questions.prompt: How are covalent bonds formed?
questions.why: Covalent bonds involve shared electron pairs.
questions.trap: Electron transfer is ionic bonding.

questions.prompt: In chemistry, what does pH refer to?
questions.why: pH reflects hydrogen ion concentration in a solution.
questions.trap: It does not measure heat or humidity.

questions.prompt: What do you call a compound that dissociates in water and allows the solution to conduct electricity?
questions.why: Electrolytes form ions in solution which carry charge.
questions.trap: Buffers resist pH change but aren't defined by conductivity.

questions.prompt: What is the process called when complex compounds are broken down to release energy?
questions.why: Catabolism breaks molecules down and releases energy.
questions.trap: Anabolism builds molecules and requires energy input.

questions.prompt: How is the atomic weight (mass number) most directly determined?

choices.A: By adding the number of protons and neutrons

questions.answer: By adding the number of protons and neutrons
questions.why: Most mass is in the nucleus: protons + neutrons.
questions.trap: Electrons contribute very little to mass.

questions.prompt: What type of solution has a higher concentration of solutes than the fluid inside the cell?
questions.why: Hypertonic means higher solute concentration outside the cell.
questions.trap: Hypotonic is lower; isotonic is equal.

questions.prompt: What is the primary function of mitochondria?
questions.why: Mitochondria generate ATP through cellular respiration.
questions.trap: Packaging proteins is Golgi; DNA storage is nucleus.

questions.prompt: What is the chief function of the Golgi apparatus?

choices.A: Modify and package proteins/lipids for transport

questions.answer: Modify and package proteins/lipids for transport
questions.why: Golgi modifies, sorts, and ships in vesicles.
questions.trap: Digesting debris is lysosomes; ATP is mitochondria.

questions.prompt: What does DNA stand for?
questions.why: DNA is deoxyribonucleic acid.
questions.trap: RNA is ribonucleic acid.

questions.prompt: What vesicular transport process releases substances outside the cell?
questions.why: Exocytosis exports substances by vesicle fusion with the membrane.
questions.trap: Endocytosis brings material into the cell.

questions.prompt: What type of cell division produces two genetically identical daughter cells?
questions.why: Mitosis creates identical daughter cells for growth/repair.
questions.trap: Meiosis makes gametes with half the chromosomes.

questions.prompt: During which phase of mitosis do chromosomes line up along the center of the cell?
questions.why: Chromosomes align at the metaphase plate in metaphase.
questions.trap: Anaphase is separation; telophase is re-forming nuclei.

questions.prompt: In DNA, which nitrogenous base pairs with adenine?
questions.why: A pairs with T in DNA (A pairs with U in RNA).
questions.trap: Uracil is RNA, not DNA.

questions.prompt: What passive transport process moves water and small solutes through a membrane due to hydrostatic pressure?
questions.why: Filtration is pressure-driven movement through a membrane.
questions.trap: Osmosis is water movement due to solute gradient, not pressure.

questions.prompt: What hairlike cell-surface structures beat in waves to move material along?
questions.why: Cilia move mucus/fluids across surfaces.
questions.trap: Microvilli increase surface area but do not beat.

questions.prompt: What do you call a cell that can divide and develop into different kinds of cells?
questions.why: Stem cells can self-renew and differentiate into multiple cell types.
questions.trap: Mature specialized cells generally don't differentiate into many types.

questions.prompt: Which tissue type is the most widespread and varied in the body?
questions.why: Connective tissue includes bone, cartilage, blood, adipose, and more.
questions.trap: Epithelial covers/lines but is not the most varied.

questions.prompt: What structures attach bone to bone?
questions.why: Ligaments connect bone to bone across joints.
questions.trap: Tendons connect muscle to bone.

questions.prompt: What structures attach muscle to bone?
questions.why: Tendons transmit muscle force to bone.
questions.trap: Ligaments connect bone to bone.

questions.prompt: Which tissue type lacks blood vessels and depends on diffusion from a basement membrane for nutrients?
questions.why: Epithelium is avascular and relies on diffusion from underlying connective tissue via basement membrane.
questions.trap: Muscle and nervous tissues are vascularized.

questions.prompt: What do you call glands that secrete their products into ducts?
questions.why: Exocrine glands use ducts; endocrine glands secrete into blood.
questions.trap: Apocrine/holocrine are secretion modes of some exocrine glands, not the duct vs blood distinction.

questions.prompt: What is another name for liquid connective tissue?
questions.why: Blood is considered a connective tissue with a fluid matrix (plasma).
questions.trap: Adipose and areolar are connective tissues but not liquid.

questions.prompt: What modified epithelial cells produce large quantities of mucus?
questions.why: Goblet cells secrete mucus to protect and lubricate surfaces.
questions.trap: Keratinocytes make keratin; melanocytes make melanin.

questions.prompt: Which type of cartilage is found at the ends of movable joints?
questions.why: Articular cartilage is hyaline cartilage covering bone ends in synovial joints.
questions.trap: Fibrocartilage is in discs/menisci; elastic is ear/epiglottis.

questions.prompt: What membrane surrounds each lung and lines the thoracic cavity?
questions.why: Pleura is the serous membrane of the lungs and thoracic cavity.
questions.trap: Pericardium surrounds the heart; peritoneum lines the abdominopelvic cavity.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the outermost layer of the skin?
questions.why: The epidermis is the outer epithelial layer of skin.
questions.trap: Dermis is deeper connective tissue; hypodermis is subcutaneous layer.

questions.prompt: Skin cells produce which tough, fibrous protein?
questions.why: Keratin strengthens and waterproofs the epidermis and hair/nails.
questions.trap: Collagen and elastin are abundant in dermis, not epidermal keratinization.

questions.prompt: The skin helps initiate the production of which vitamin?
questions.why: UV light triggers vitamin D synthesis in skin.
questions.trap: Vitamin C and B12 are not synthesized in skin.

questions.prompt: Which type of skin cancer is most deadly because it metastasizes quickly?
questions.why: Melanoma has high metastatic potential compared with other common skin cancers.
questions.trap: Basal cell is most common but least likely to metastasize.

questions.prompt: How does the skin help conserve body heat?

choices.A: By vasoconstricting dermal blood vessels to reduce heat loss
choices.C: By increasing evaporation from the surface
choices.D: By dilating dermal blood vessels to release heat

questions.answer: By vasoconstricting dermal blood vessels to reduce heat loss
questions.why: Vasoconstriction reduces blood flow to skin surface, decreasing heat loss.
questions.trap: Sweating and vasodilation increase heat loss.

questions.prompt: What structure in the dermis causes hair to stand on end?
questions.why: Arrector pili smooth muscle pulls hair upright (goosebumps).
questions.trap: Sebaceous glands produce sebum; hair bulb is growth region.

questions.prompt: List five major functions of the skin.
questions.correct_text: Protection; temperature regulation; sensation; excretion (sweat); vitamin D production
questions.why: These are the core functions commonly emphasized in A&P texts.
questions.hook: Protect, regulate, sense, excrete, make D.

questions.prompt: Which skin disorder results from a genetic lack of melanin?
questions.why: Albinism involves reduced/absent melanin production.
questions.trap: Jaundice is bilirubin-related; cyanosis is low oxygen.

questions.prompt: What role does melanin play in the skin?
questions.correct_text: It provides pigmentation and helps protect DNA from UV radiation by absorbing UV light.
questions.why: Melanin absorbs UV, reducing sun damage.

questions.prompt: What do you call the creation of new bone?
questions.why: Ossification (osteogenesis) is bone formation.
questions.trap: Resorption is bone breakdown by osteoclasts.

questions.prompt: What is the basic structural unit of compact bone called?
questions.why: Osteons (Haversian systems) are the structural units of compact bone.
questions.trap: Sarcomere is muscle; alveolus is lung.

questions.prompt: What is the cartilage growth plate between the epiphysis and diaphysis called?
questions.why: The epiphyseal plate is where lengthwise bone growth occurs.
questions.trap: Periosteum is outer membrane; endosteum lines internal surfaces.

questions.prompt: In long bones, what is the head region called?
questions.why: Epiphyses are the ends (head regions) of long bones.
questions.trap: Diaphysis is the shaft.

questions.prompt: Where are red blood cells produced?
questions.why: Hematopoiesis occurs in red marrow.
questions.trap: Yellow marrow mainly stores fat.

questions.prompt: What type of fracture occurs when the bone pierces the skin?
questions.why: An open/compound fracture breaks through the skin.
questions.trap: Closed fractures do not pierce skin.

questions.prompt: A fetus’s first skeleton is primarily composed of what?
questions.why: Most fetal skeletal structures are cartilage models later ossified.
questions.trap: Bone forms later via ossification.

questions.prompt: Which disorder literally means “porous bone”?
questions.why: Osteoporosis involves loss of bone density (porous bone).
questions.trap: Osteomyelitis is infection; osteoarthritis is joint wear; osteomalacia is soft bones.

questions.prompt: Which bone cells dissolve unwanted or unhealthy bone?
questions.why: Osteoclasts resorb bone; osteoblasts build bone.
questions.trap: Osteoblasts are the opposite function.

questions.prompt: What dense, fibrous membrane covers the diaphysis of a long bone?
questions.why: Periosteum covers the external surface of bones (except articular cartilage areas).
questions.trap: Endosteum lines internal cavities.

questions.prompt: What is a lateral curvature of the spine called?
questions.why: Scoliosis is lateral curvature.
questions.trap: Kyphosis and lordosis are exaggerated curves in sagittal plane.

questions.prompt: How many pairs of true ribs do humans have?
questions.why: Ribs 1–7 attach directly to the sternum (true ribs).
questions.trap: 8–10 are false ribs; 11–12 are floating.

questions.prompt: Which part of the os coxae supports your weight while sitting?
questions.why: The ischial tuberosities bear weight when seated.
questions.trap: Ilium is upper wing; pubis is anterior.

questions.prompt: Which bone forms the heel?
questions.why: The calcaneus is the heel bone.
questions.trap: Talus sits above calcaneus at ankle joint.

questions.prompt: The pituitary gland rests in an indentation of the sphenoid bone called what?
questions.why: The sella turcica houses the pituitary gland.
questions.trap: Foramen magnum is in occipital bone; mastoid is temporal bone.

questions.prompt: List the three arches of the foot.
questions.correct_text: Medial longitudinal arch; lateral longitudinal arch; transverse arch

questions.prompt: What is the gel-like core of an intervertebral disc called?
questions.why: The nucleus pulposus is the gel center; annulus fibrosus is the fibrous outer ring.
questions.trap: Annulus fibrosus is the outer layer, not the core.

questions.prompt: What are the soft spots on an infant’s skull called?
questions.why: Fontanels are the soft membranous gaps between cranial bones in infants.
questions.trap: Sutures are fibrous joints between skull bones.

questions.prompt: What is an exaggerated thoracic curve called?
questions.why: Kyphosis is exaggerated posterior thoracic curve.
questions.trap: Lordosis is exaggerated lumbar anterior curve.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the second cervical vertebra (C2)?
questions.why: C1 is atlas; C2 is axis.
questions.trap: Atlas is the first cervical vertebra.

questions.prompt: The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) helps prevent which motion at the knee?

choices.A: Anterior sliding of the tibia relative to the femur
choices.B: Posterior sliding of the tibia relative to the femur

questions.answer: Anterior sliding of the tibia relative to the femur
questions.why: ACL primarily resists anterior translation of the tibia.
questions.trap: PCL resists posterior tibial translation.
questions.hook: ACL stops tibia from going forward.

questions.prompt: What fluid fills a synovial joint capsule and lubricates the joint?
questions.why: Synovial fluid lubricates and nourishes articular cartilage.
questions.trap: CSF is around brain/spinal cord.

questions.prompt: What is another common name for a joint?
questions.why: A joint is an articulation where bones meet.
questions.trap: Foramen is a hole; fossa is a depression.

questions.prompt: Which type of arthritis is primarily due to wear and tear?
questions.why: Osteoarthritis is degenerative 'wear-and-tear' arthritis.
questions.trap: Rheumatoid is autoimmune inflammation.

questions.prompt: Which type of arthritis is an autoimmune disease?
questions.why: Rheumatoid arthritis is driven by autoimmune inflammation of synovium.
questions.trap: Osteoarthritis is degenerative, not autoimmune.

questions.prompt: Which joint category is also known as amphiarthroses?
questions.why: Amphiarthroses allow slight movement.
questions.trap: Diarthroses are freely movable; synarthroses are immovable.

questions.prompt: Which joint category is also known as diarthroses?
questions.why: Diarthroses are freely movable joints (typically synovial).
questions.trap: Amphiarthroses are slightly movable.

questions.prompt: What movement straightens a joint and increases the angle between bones?
questions.why: Extension increases joint angle; flexion decreases it.
questions.trap: Flexion is the opposite movement.

questions.prompt: What movement points the toes/foot downward?
questions.why: Plantar flexion points the foot down; dorsiflexion lifts it up.
questions.trap: Dorsiflexion is toes up.

questions.prompt: Which joint is most likely to be dislocated?
questions.why: The shoulder has great mobility but less stability, so dislocation is common.
questions.trap: Hip is more stable due to deep socket.

questions.prompt: What is a single muscle cell called?
questions.why: A muscle fiber is a single muscle cell; fascicles are bundles of fibers.
questions.trap: Sarcomere is a unit inside the fiber.

questions.prompt: Starting at the neuromuscular junction, describe the basic steps of skeletal muscle contraction.
questions.correct_text: ACh released → ACh binds receptors → action potential spreads on sarcolemma/T-tubules → SR releases Ca2+ → Ca2+ binds troponin → tropomyosin shifts → myosin binds actin → power stroke → ATP binds to detach → ATP hydrolysis re-cocks myosin; Ca2+ pumped back to SR for relaxation.
questions.hook: ACh → AP → Ca2+ → cross-bridge → ATP reset.

questions.prompt: What is the individual contractile unit of a muscle called?
questions.why: Sarcomeres are the repeating contractile units in myofibrils.
questions.trap: Osteon is bone; alveolus is lung.

questions.prompt: Identify the three types of muscle and one key characteristic of each.
questions.correct_text: Skeletal: striated, voluntary; Cardiac: striated, involuntary, intercalated discs; Smooth: nonstriated, involuntary, in walls of organs/vessels.

questions.prompt: What is the short-burst energy storage form inside muscle?
questions.why: Phosphocreatine rapidly regenerates ATP for short bursts.
questions.trap: ATP is immediate energy but not stored in large amounts.

questions.prompt: At rest, muscles obtain most of their energy by metabolizing what substance?
questions.why: At rest and low intensity, muscles primarily use fatty acids aerobically.
questions.trap: Glucose becomes more dominant at higher intensity.

questions.prompt: During muscle contraction, the sarcoplasmic reticulum releases what substance?
questions.why: Ca2+ release from SR enables actin-myosin interaction.
questions.trap: ACh is released from motor neurons, not SR.

questions.prompt: What is a single, brief muscle contraction called?
questions.why: A twitch is one brief contraction-relaxation cycle.
questions.trap: Tetany is sustained contraction.

questions.prompt: What chemical messenger is released from a motor neuron at the neuromuscular junction?
questions.why: ACh triggers depolarization at the neuromuscular junction.
questions.trap: Epinephrine is a hormone/neurotransmitter but not the NMJ primary transmitter.

questions.prompt: What type of contraction occurs when muscle length changes while tension stays about the same?
questions.why: Isotonic contractions change muscle length; isometric keeps length constant.
questions.trap: Isometric is the opposite: tension changes without length change.

questions.prompt: Which type of neuron detects stimuli and transmits impulses to the brain and spinal cord?
questions.why: Afferent neurons carry information toward the CNS.
questions.trap: Efferent neurons carry commands away from CNS.

questions.prompt: Which division of the nervous system controls the body’s automatic activities?
questions.why: ANS regulates involuntary functions like heart rate and digestion.
questions.trap: Somatic controls voluntary skeletal muscle.

questions.prompt: Which type of neuron carries messages from the brain to muscles or gland cells?
questions.why: Efferent neurons carry output from CNS to effectors.
questions.trap: Astrocytes are glial support cells.

questions.prompt: What are the impulse-conducting cells of the nervous system called?
questions.why: Neurons conduct electrical impulses; neuroglia support.
questions.trap: Neuroglia do not primarily conduct impulses.

questions.prompt: Which division of the nervous system carries signals from the organs to the CNS?
questions.why: Afferent pathways bring sensory input to the CNS.
questions.trap: Efferent sends commands to effectors.

questions.prompt: What are the supportive cells of the nervous system called?
questions.why: Glial cells support, nourish, and protect neurons.
questions.trap: Dendrites are neuron parts, not support cells.

questions.prompt: Which type of neuroglia helps form the blood-brain barrier?
questions.why: Astrocyte end-feet contribute to BBB structure and regulation.
questions.trap: Ependymal cells line ventricles and help with CSF.

questions.prompt: Which brain structure contains more neurons than any other part of the brain?
questions.why: The cerebellum contains a very high density of neurons.
questions.trap: Cerebrum is larger but has fewer total neurons than cerebellum.

questions.prompt: Which brain structure is crucial for converting short-term memory into long-term memory?
questions.why: The hippocampus is key for memory consolidation.
questions.trap: Hypothalamus is homeostasis control, not memory consolidation.

questions.prompt: Which brain structure contains control centers for hunger, thirst, and temperature regulation?
questions.why: Hypothalamus regulates many homeostatic drives.
questions.trap: Thalamus is a relay station; pituitary is endocrine output.

questions.prompt: Which receptors are responsible for color vision?
questions.why: Cones detect color; rods are for low-light vision.
questions.trap: Rods do not detect color.

questions.prompt: What is clouding of the lens of the eye called?
questions.why: A cataract is opacity/clouding of the lens.
questions.trap: Glaucoma is increased intraocular pressure.

questions.prompt: What condition is caused by increased intraocular pressure?
questions.why: Glaucoma involves elevated intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve.
questions.trap: Cataract is lens clouding, not pressure.

questions.prompt: Where is the smallest bone in the body located?
questions.why: The stapes is the smallest bone and is in the middle ear.
questions.trap: Carpals are small, but not the smallest bone.

questions.prompt: What is another name for pain receptors?
questions.why: Nociceptors detect painful stimuli.
questions.trap: Mechanoreceptors detect touch/pressure; photoreceptors detect light.

questions.prompt: Which receptor type helps you orient your body in space?
questions.why: Proprioceptors provide information about body position and movement.
questions.trap: Baroreceptors detect blood pressure.

questions.prompt: List the five basic tastes.
Describe the pain pathway in simple terms.
questions.correct_text: Nociceptors activated → sensory neuron to dorsal horn of spinal cord → ascending pathways (e.g., spinothalamic) → thalamus → cerebral cortex perception, with modulation from brainstem/endorphins.

questions.prompt: Which cranial nerve is involved in hearing?
questions.why: CN VIII carries auditory and balance information.
questions.trap: CN VII controls facial expression; CN V is facial sensation/chewing.

questions.prompt: What is the sense of smell called?
questions.why: Olfaction is smell; gustation is taste.
questions.trap: Gustation is taste.
